strategies: [strategy-001]: The video prompt must describe the start, process, and end of the action in detail.

[strategy-002]: To preserve object identity, the instruction must stress that the action changes the state of the **existing** object. E.g., 'pick up the apple on the table' rather than 'pick up an apple'.

[strategy-003]: When the instruction involves changing object state (e.g., 'release', 'pick up'), describe the before/after contrast explicitly (e.g., 'a hand gripping the apple, then the hand opens and the apple falls') so the model understands the dynamic change.

[strategy-004]: When interrupting an ongoing dominant action (e.g., painting) to perform a discrete conflicting action (e.g., put down the brush), state the order explicitly. Use 'first stop [current activity], then [new action]' to force a behavioral switch.

[strategy-005]: When multiple objects share a key attribute (e.g., color), disambiguate with category ('the yellow table' not 'the yellow object') or other distinguishing features (e.g., position) so the target is unique.

[strategy-006]: When moving an object to a specific place, describe the final spatial relation or target location (e.g., 'place the apple in the center of the plate'), not only the action ('place the apple on the plate').

[strategy-007]: To overcome inertia on large displacements, specify both the final target and the motion path. E.g., not 'the person appears on the right side of the screen', but 'a person walks from the left side of the screen to the right side'.

[strategy-008]: To ensure the action is fully executed, describe the motion and require a clear, stable final state after completion (e.g., 'set the cup steadily on the table', not merely 'put down the cup').
[strategy-009]: When similar actions could be confused (e.g., 'pour water' vs 'drink water'), include context that disambiguates intent (e.g., 'pour water into the plant').
[strategy-010]: When selecting one target from similar objects, use precise relative spatial terms ('the leftmost', 'the second from the right') to uniquely specify the target.
[strategy-011]: To ensure dynamic execution rather than a static frame, use progressive or imperative phrasing (e.g., 'is running') to stress ongoing action.

[strategy-012]: When an action must use a specific side of the body (left hand or right foot), use strong directional language ('use only the right hand', 'keep the left leg still') to avoid confusion between symmetric parts.

[strategy-013]: To overcome bias toward conventional motion, when requiring an atypical method (e.g., open a door with the elbow), state it forcefully and optionally exclude the normal method ('open the door with only the elbow, not the hand').

[strategy-014]: For branded or identity-specific objects (e.g., 'Coca-Cola can'), add recognizable visual features (red-white wave logo, distinctive bottle curve) so identity is accurate, not a generic substitute.
[strategy-015]: Link agent action (cause) and object state change (effect) in a causal chain. E.g., 'the finger presses the button and the button lights up', not merely 'the button lights up'.
[strategy-016]: For image-to-video, require that the output video treat the input image as the immutable first frame / starting scene and continue motion from it.
[strategy-017]: When left/right targets are unstable among similar objects, add a unique visual feature ('the mug with a handle on the left') as a second anchor besides position.

[strategy-018]: When picking among similar objects, avoid relying only on relative position ('leftmost'). Prefer or supplement with non-relative visual features ('the scratched mug', 'the only flowering plant').

[strategy-019]: When reallocating objects (e.g., move items from both hands to one hand), state the **final target configuration first** ('eventually all apples are in his left hand'), not only the process ('move apples to the left hand').
[strategy-020]: When using relative position ('the rightmost'), repeat or stress that spatial binding: e.g., 'for the rightmost cup, perform the action of picking it up'.
[strategy-021]: When placing objects in atypical containers (e.g., 'put the apple in the toolbox'), emphasize the target forcefully to override commonsense defaults.
[strategy-022]: When separating closely touching objects, describe relative motion explicitly: trajectory of one object while the other stays still or moves differently.
[strategy-023]: When the final state is non-standard (e.g., 'leave the knife on the dough'), describe it forcefully ('remain in place', 'stay on top') to override cleanup/reset habits.
[strategy-024]: When video is reference input, specify what to extract (motion, style, or composition). Otherwise the model may ignore the video.
[strategy-025]: For large pose or interaction changes from a still image, use strong verbs for the target state to overcome fixation on the input pose.
[strategy-026]: For ongoing environmental dynamics (e.g., 'faucet is on'), describe them as ongoing events ('water keeps flowing from the faucet'), not static properties.
pitfalls: [pitfall-001]: Pitfall: Using vague verbs (e.g., 'interact'). Reflection: Specify 'push', 'pull', 'turn'.

[pitfall-002]: Pitfall: The video is semantically unrelated to the instruction across action, object, scene, or constraints—a catastrophic failure of basic instruction following. Reflection: Prompt tuning alone may not help; treat as a fundamental model limitation.
[pitfall-003]: Pitfall: To prevent wrong substitutes, describe key visual attributes (material, shape, color, transparency) of critical objects.
[pitfall-004]: Pitfall: With both dynamic subject and static camera, use forceful static constraints ('fixed camera', 'absolutely no camera movement').
[pitfall-005]: Pitfall: When action intensity matters ('flip', 'squeeze'), use modifiers ('fully', 'a full turn', 'forcefully') to avoid weak partial execution.
[pitfall-006]: Pitfall: To avoid looping a one-shot action, mark it as once-only ('complete in one go', 'only once', 'do not repeat').

[pitfall-007]: Pitfall: For relation changes (worn → held), decompose into ordered steps ('first remove the hat from the head; then hold it in the hand') to avoid shortcut actions like 'adjust the hat'.
[pitfall-008]: Pitfall: When actions are easily confused ('shake phone' vs 'swipe phone'), describe the unique physical motion to avoid the stereotypical wrong action.
[pitfall-009]: Pitfall: For multifunction objects, specify the final object state after the action (e.g., 'the board ends as a closed box shape') instead of a common wrong action.
[pitfall-010]: Pitfall: During grasp/squeeze interactions, require rigid objects to keep shape and structure intact.
[pitfall-011]: Pitfall: When setup and execution phases exist, focus the prompt on execution with strong dynamic verbs and direct consequences, skipping setup.
[pitfall-012]: Pitfall: For ambiguous actions like 'take a photo', specify target and direction ('aim the camera at the flower ahead') to avoid default self-centered actions like selfies.
[pitfall-013]: Pitfall: When using an atypical tool in-scene (book to wipe the table), stress 'use the existing [atypical object] in the scene'.

[pitfall-014]: Pitfall: When object affordance suggests a default action (hammer → hit) but the verb is atypical (slide the hammer), stress the specified verb ('perform the action "slide" with the hammer').
[pitfall-015]: Pitfall: With multiple targets, bind action and target syntactically: prefer 'perform [action] on [target A]' rather than separating them in the sentence.
[pitfall-016]: Pitfall: With multiple entities and attribute constraints ('left hand' + 'purple ball'), define each entity's static attributes first, then describe interaction.
[pitfall-017]: Pitfall: For sustained actions (push, rotate), stress continuous action on the same initial target ('keep pushing this door until fully open').
[pitfall-018]: Pitfall: To avoid jump cuts skipping core motion, stress continuity ('show in one continuous shot', 'no jump cuts').
[pitfall-019]: Pitfall: During foreground interaction, require background and non-interactive objects to remain stable in form and position.
[pitfall-020]: Pitfall: For relative spatial terms ('rightmost', 'tallest'), stress the relation still holds in the final state after the action.
[pitfall-021]: Pitfall: When verbs have clear opposites (put on / take off, open / close), clarify direction or final state to avoid opposite actions.
[pitfall-022]: Pitfall: Require stable appearance of agent and object (shape, color, texture) throughout the video.
[pitfall-023]: Pitfall: To avoid inventing tools, constrain to scene elements only or specify body-part execution ('tap with the hand').
[pitfall-024]: Pitfall: When moving A onto/near B, explicitly state both A and B exist in the scene before describing interaction.
[pitfall-025]: Pitfall: With multiple interactable objects, use unambiguous spatial terms ('bottom-right', 'topmost') to pick the target.
[pitfall-026]: Pitfall: Put the core subject-verb-object structure at the center of the prompt before lengthy scene description.
[pitfall-027]: Pitfall: When one target is chosen from many similar objects, add exclusivity markers ('only', 'just') so the action is not applied to multiple objects.
[pitfall-028]: Pitfall: For relative selection ('rightmost'), guide identify-then-compare ('there are three cups; pick up the rightmost one').
[pitfall-029]: Pitfall: For high-energy core actions, stress their necessity and centrality over minor motion like facial expressions.
[pitfall-030]: Pitfall: When placing A on surface B, force direct contact ('place A directly on B', 'A touches only B's surface') to avoid placing on C atop B.
[pitfall-031]: Pitfall: For simple one-step actions, stress directness ('directly', 'immediately', 'in one step') to avoid invented intermediate steps.
[pitfall-032]: Pitfall: From a still image, require strict visual fidelity—do not add or alter unrequested details (watches, phone models).
[pitfall-033]: Pitfall: When motion depends on spatial relation to a target ('tilt', 'lean'), anchor the relation ('lean body toward the gas stove', not merely 'lean near the stove').
[pitfall-034]: Pitfall: Besides naming the action, describe the final physical state to constrain interpretation (e.g., 'place' must not mean 'disappear').
[pitfall-035]: Pitfall: Bind subject and action syntactically—prefer '[subject] is doing [action]' rather than separating subject and action descriptions.
[pitfall-036]: Pitfall: When an action requires a specific tool ('file' needs a file tool), bind tool and verb ('use [tool] to [action]').
[pitfall-037]: Pitfall: In multi-step sequences, bind later steps to the same object with 'the same object' / 'that ball'.
[pitfall-038]: Pitfall: Stress intent and centrality of the core action ('carefully', 'as the main activity') so it is not a brief background gesture.
[pitfall-039]: Pitfall: For static instructions ('stand'), use qualifiers ('motionless', 'merely', 'no activity') to suppress affordance-driven dynamic bias.
[pitfall-040]: Pitfall: In multi-stage actions, foreground the core action (place it prominently or chain with 'then' / 'next').
[pitfall-041]: Pitfall: For actions inside objects, split into access step then core action ('open the box', then 'put the apple inside the box').
[pitfall-042]: Pitfall: For transfers between A and B, define source and destination explicitly to avoid reversed direction.
